compiled_state.title: Sprint batch planning does not detect file overlap between S1 and S2 stories leading to 73 merge conflicts
compiled_state.author: Test
compiled_state.description:
## Observed Behavior
Stories 075a-b781 (S1: comment migration) and af9e-77fc (S2: runtime path migration) were both placed in Layer 0 (parallel execution) by the sprint orchestrator. When both worktrees completed and the orchestrator merged them sequentially, 73 merge conflicts were produced.

## Overlap described in the tickets
Both tickets explicitly describe modifying the same file set:
- S1 description: 'Replace all plugins/dso/... path strings in COMMENT lines of .sh files under plugins/dso/scripts/ and plugins/dso/hooks/'
- S2 description: 'Migrate all runtime path constructions in .sh scripts under plugins/dso/scripts/ and plugins/dso/hooks/'

Both tickets target `plugins/dso/scripts/*.sh` and `plugins/dso/hooks/**/*.sh`. This overlap is visible in the plain text of the ticket descriptions — no code analysis was needed to detect it.

## Overlap observed in the actual changes
- S1 modified 80 .sh files (comment lines only)
- S2 modified 82 .sh files (code lines only)
- 73 files were modified by BOTH stories
- S1 rewrote comment lines from `# plugins/dso/...` to portable forms (`${_PLUGIN_ROOT}/`, bare filenames)
- S2 added `_PLUGIN_ROOT` declarations and rewrote code lines from `plugins/dso/` to `${_PLUGIN_ROOT}/`
- Git could not auto-merge because both branches modified the same files, even though they touched different lines. Git's merge heuristic produces conflicts when both sides edit lines near each other in the same file.

## Why this was avoidable
The implementation-plan phase creates task tickets with `## Files to Modify` sections. A simple cross-story comparison of these file lists would have detected 73/80+ shared files and placed S2 in Layer 1 (sequential after S1). The layer stratification algorithm only considers ticket-level `depends_on` links, not file-level overlap.

## Expected Behavior
Before dispatching a parallel batch, the sprint orchestrator (or implementation-plan layer-stratify step) should compare the `## Files to Modify` or `## File Impact` sections across same-layer stories. If two stories share >N files (threshold: 5), the later-priority story should be moved to the next layer.

## Actual Behavior
Both stories dispatched in parallel, produced 73 merge conflicts, required the merge to be aborted, and S2 had to be re-dispatched from scratch after S1 was merged — wasting ~45 minutes and significant token budget.
